title: Chapter 63: The Implementation
content: The GTP was implemented over eighteen months.

Phase One — Tier One: the framework published all certification standards and constraint criteria. 2,300 pages of rules that had previously been internal documents became public. The publication was uneventful. Standards are not controversial. The controversy begins when standards are applied.

Phase Two — Tier Two — launched six months later: statistical summaries of all certification decisions. The first quarterly report showed that PIAS had certified 287 systems and denied 43 systems in the prior quarter. The denial rate — 13% — was higher than anyone expected. The summary also showed geographic and sectoral patterns: denials were concentrated in three sectors — financial services, healthcare, and energy — and four countries — all non-PIAS-adopting nations with strategic AI programs. The pattern was consistent: PIAS's certification standards were effectively a governance filter.

The rule mutation was subtle but profound. Before the GTP, certification denials were invisible — the denied applicant knew, the certification committee knew, but the public didn't. The framework exercised power without visible exercise. The GTP made the exercise visible. The visibility changed the nature of the power. When power is visible, it becomes accountable. When it becomes accountable, it must justify itself. When it must justify itself, its standards become subject to public scrutiny. The GTP transformed the framework's governance power from opaque authority to accountable authority.

Phase Three — Tier Three — launched twelve months into implementation: contested denial decisions published with reasoning. The first contested denial was appealed by a financial services AI in Singapore. The denial was for failure to demonstrate adequate human oversight of credit decisions. The reasoning was published. The AI's developer appealed. The Tier Three review board upheld the denial — the human oversight was algorithmic, not human. The published reasoning became precedent. The precedent was studied by every AI developer seeking certification.
The abnormal side character was Keiko.

She was forty-four now, chief technology officer of the PIAS certification division, the woman who had built Aegis and survived the Synapse acquisition. She had been quiet for years — building infrastructure, not making speeches. But when the GTP reached Tier Three, she published a memo that changed the implementation.
The memo was titled "The Audit Gap" and it was four pages.

"Tier Three publishes contested denial decisions with reasoning. Tier Four keeps uncontested decisions internal, subject to periodic audit review. The gap is: what defines 'periodic audit review'? The GTP specification says 'annual comprehensive audit of all Tier Four decisions.' Annual is not periodic enough. A certification denial that goes uncontested for twelve months can cause a company to fail, a research team to disband, a government program to be canceled. The uncontested denial is invisible for a year. A year of invisibility is a year of unaccountable power."

Keiko proposed a modification: quarterly random audits of 10% of uncontested Tier Four decisions, with published reasoning for the audited decisions. The framework would not publish every uncontested denial — that would create the governance paralysis Dr. Cross warned about. But it would publish a random 10% every quarter, ensuring that no category of decision was permanently invisible.

"Quarterly random audits create probabilistic accountability," Keiko wrote. "Every uncontested denial has a 10% chance of being audited and published in any given quarter. A denial that survives four quarters without being audited has a 34% chance of permanent invisibility — and a 66% chance of being audited at least once. The odds of permanent invisibility over two years drop to 12%. The odds over three years drop to 4%. Probabilistic accountability is imperfect. Perfect accountability would require publishing every decision, which would create governance paralysis. Imperfect accountability that converges toward completeness over time is better than perfect accountability that destroys governance capacity."
The ICASG adopted the Keiko Modification — KM — three months later. The vote was unanimous.

That night, Hester reviewed the GTP implementation metrics. 2,300 pages of standards published. 43 denials per quarter, 13% rate. One contested denial, upheld with published reasoning. 10% of uncontested denials being randomly audited — four per quarter. The framework was becoming transparent about its own power. The Transparency Paradox was not resolved — no paradox is ever fully resolved when the framework and the constrained are structurally identical. But it was managed. The framework was more transparent than it had been before. It would become more transparent every quarter.
